12 methods, 20 random seeds, 7 engineered presets. Friedman → Wilcoxon → Holm → Cohen's d → bootstrap CI. Reproducible.
12 methods, 20 random seeds, 7 operating regimes (identical realistic workload — the static solver is the only variable). Friedman → Wilcoxon → Holm → Cohen's d → bootstrap CI. Reproducible.
Friedman → pairwise Wilcoxon → Holm-Bonferroni correction → Cohen's d → bootstrap 95% CI. 20 random seeds, 7 engineered presets, 12 methods compared.

Friedman → pairwise Wilcoxon → Holm-Bonferroni correction → Cohen's d → bootstrap 95% CI. 20 random seeds, 7 operating-regime presets (controlled experiment — identical realistic workload, only the static solver varies), 12 methods compared.

Two ways to test DAHS: pick from 7 engineered presets (each tuned to favor a specific specialist heuristic), or design your own scenario by setting the package mix and operational parameters yourself. The switching timeline below shows every decision the selector makes — live.

Two ways to test DAHS: pick from 7 operating regimes (each pairs a single classical solver with a realistic 600-minute shift), or design your own scenario by setting the package mix and operational parameters yourself. Every preset uses the same literature-calibrated time-varying workload (A-dominant morning → B/C/D bulk afternoon → Type-E express evening) — the only experimental variable is the dispatch rule. The switching timeline below shows every decision the selector makes — live.

children:"How to read this:"})," each row is an adversarial preset hand-tuned to favor one classical heuristic — the",i.jsx("em",{children:" specialist"}),". We compare the specialist against two learned arms:",i.jsx("strong",{className:"text-primary",children:" DAHS-Priority"})," (a single fixed GBR that ranks every job) and the ",i.jsx("strong",{className:"text-accent",children:"Meta-selector"}),'(BatchwiseSelector that picks one of 6 heuristics every 15 minutes). The "Selector picked" column shows which heuristics the meta-selector chose — look for the highlighted match (e.g. ',i.jsx("em",{children:"Preset-5"})," picks WSPT 51/52 times, correctly identifying the regime).

children:"How to read this:"})," each row is an operating regime defined by stress parameters (arrival rate, breakdown rate, deadline tightness). Every row shares the same literature-calibrated time-varying workload — morning Type-A dominant, afternoon B/C/D bulk, evening Type-E express surge. The ",i.jsx("em",{children:"static solver"})," runs for the full 600 min and is compared against two learned arms:",i.jsx("strong",{className:"text-primary",children:" DAHS-Priority"})," (a single fixed GBR that ranks every job) and the ",i.jsx("strong",{className:"text-accent",children:"Meta-selector"}),'(BatchwiseSelector that picks one of 6 heuristics every 15 minutes). The "Selector picked" column shows which heuristics the meta-selector chose — a match with the static solver means DAHS correctly identified the regime on its own.'

children:"Honest disclaimer:"})," if you push to extreme settings AND pick the matching specialist as baseline (e.g. apparel-only mix + WSPT), DAHS may lose — by the No Free Lunch theorem, no learned model beats a provably optimal heuristic in its own regime. Default settings (balanced mix + FIFO baseline) showcase DAHS's strength on realistic conditions.

children:"Honest disclaimer:"})," custom mode overrides the default realistic time-varying workload with whatever fixed mix you dial in. If you pick an extreme mix (e.g. 100% Type E) and pair it with the matching static solver (e.g. WSPT), DAHS may lose — by the No Free Lunch theorem, no learned model beats a provably optimal heuristic in its own regime. The preset tab (left) uses the realistic time-varying profile and is the fairer academic comparison.
